{"step_by_step_thinking": "Red blood cells (RBCs) do not perform aerobic respiration and lack mitochondria, so they cannot use fatty acids, ketone bodies, or amino acids like alanine for energy. Instead, RBCs rely exclusively on glycolysis to produce ATP, and their primary energy source is glucose. This is supported by multiple documents, including Document [7], which states that 'the testes, renal medulla, and erythrocytes cannot survive long periods without glucose.' Similarly, Document [11] notes that'some tissues in the human body rely almost exclusively on glucose as a metabolic fuel source,' and specifically mentions erythrocytes (RBCs) as one of them. In fasting states, although ketone bodies become more available to the brain and other tissues, RBCs continue to depend on glucose. There is no evidence in the documents that RBCs use alanine, ketone bodies, or fatty acids as energy sources. Therefore, glucose is the only correct option.", "answer_choice": "A"}
